ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic chicken curry entry (FOODON_00002809); no specific Kongunadu-style entry exists. Closest parent match used for the curry component; rice component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken curry with rice — composite dish, approximate FoodEx2 anchor for South Asian chicken curry with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice. A00GJ (mixed dish with meat and cereal) used as the closest defensible composite anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to this specific regional Indian dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed white rice and 150 g Kongunadu-style chicken curry with gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu With Steamed Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu With Steamed Rice (cr_id G00010993). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable South Indian chicken curry with coconut at approximately 1.2 g/100g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB for this dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based curry dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, median ~65) as the dominant carbohydrate source (approximately 25 g carbohydrate per 100 g of the combined dish). The chicken curry gravy component contributes protein and fat that moderately attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (65 × 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5) / 100 = 22.9 g GL. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish is recommended for clinical precision.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g with potassium 327 mg/100g providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is well within the DASH diet threshold of 400 mg/100g, and the high potassium content (327 mg/100g) actively supports vascular tone; however, patients should request reduced-salt preparation and limit coconut quantity to manage saturated fat intake, which can independently elevate cardiovascular risk in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (~22.9 GL per 350 g serving) driven by white rice as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 65 and GL of 22.9 per standard serving place this dish in the moderate concern range for Type 2 Diabetes; patients should limit portion size to 200–250 g, increase the chicken-to-rice ratio, or substitute brown rice to reduce the glycaemic response, while benefiting from the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and chicken protein that slow gastric emptying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100g), high dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), and anti-inflammatory spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat, negligible sugar (0.21 g/100g), and a spice blend including turmeric (curcumin) and black pepper (piperine) with evidence-based anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties; patients with CVD should request reduced coconut in the gravy to further limit saturated fat and ensure chicken skin is removed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, black pepper, fennel) with lean chicken protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric (curcumin) and black pepper (piperine enhances curcumin bioavailability), ginger, and fennel provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory polyphenols; however, nightshade ingredients (tomato, green chilli) may aggravate RA in sensitive individuals and chicken skin should be removed to minimise saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice is a traditional South Indian composite dish originating from the Kongunadu region of Tamil Nadu, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian food establishments. The dish consists of a richly spiced chicken curry (kozhi kuzhambu) served alongside steamed white rice. The curry is prepared by slow-cooking bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a gravy base of tomatoes, onions, and freshly ground coconut, tempered with whole spices including black pepper, cumin, fennel, and curry leaves, and seasoned with turmeric, coriander, and green chilli. The resulting gravy is thick, deeply aromatic, and carries a characteristic peppery heat that distinguishes Kongunadu cuisine from other South Indian curry styles. The steamed white rice provides a neutral, fluffy base that absorbs the spiced gravy. Nutritionally, the combined dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notably high at 3.7 g/100g, contributed by the spice-laden gravy and rice. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100g, well-balanced by potassium at 327 mg/100g. The spice profile — particularly turmeric (curcumin) and black pepper (piperine) — confers anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular health. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, the moderate glycaemic load (~22.9 GL per 350 g serving) warrants portion control for Type 2 Diabetes patients, while the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio supports hypertension management. This dish is suitable for lunch and dinner and is a staple of Tamil Indian cuisine in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010993, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable South Indian chicken curry with coconut. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for boiled white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Multilingual names verified for Tamil, Hindi, Malay, and Chinese. Overall confidence is high given the exact HPB source match for the primary nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable South Indian chicken curry with coconut, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, most vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this traditional Kongunadu preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish entry; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source, likely reflecting the combined dish average where the rice component dilutes the chicken cholesterol contribution to a negligible level per 100g as reported. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); direct GI measurement of this specific dish is recommended for clinical precision. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice in any current ontology system.
